1.texto_original: The word which came unto Jeremiah from Jehovah, when Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, and all his army, and all the kingdoms of the earth that were under his dominion, and all the peoples, were fighting against Jerusalem, and against all the cities thereof, saying:
1.normal:
Devotional Study of Jeremiah 34:1

The first verse establishes the foundation of all biblical revelation: God as sovereign Creator. The phrase "In the beginning" places us at the inaugural moment of creation, not as an event within time, but as the beginning of time itself. God is not part of creation, but its absolute origin.

The verb "created" (from the Hebrew bara) is exclusive to God in the Scriptures, indicating creation ex nihilo (from nothing). This is not about transforming pre-existing matter, but calling into existence what did not exist before. The expression "the heavens and the earth" encompasses the totality of created reality.

This verse answers fundamental questions: Where do we come from? Who is in control? The answer is clear: everything comes from God and depends on Him. As believers, this truth gives us security in divine sovereignty over our lives.

"The word which came unto Jeremiah from Jehovah, when Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, and all his army, and all the kingdoms of the earth that were under his dominion, and all the peoples, were fighting against Jerusalem, and against all the cities thereof, saying:"
1.academico:
Exegetical Analysis of Jeremiah 34:1

Philological Analysis

The original Hebrew text presents crucial elements for exegesis. Bereshit (בְּרֵאשִׁית) is a prepositional construction meaning "in the beginning of", indicating the absolute beginning. The verb bara (בָּרָא) appears in perfect tense (qatal), denoting completed action. This verb has God as its exclusive subject in the Tanakh, distinguishing it from asah (to make) and yatsar (to form).

Historical Context

Source criticism identifies this opening with the Priestly tradition (P), dated to the exilic or post-exilic period (6th-5th century BCE). This contrasts with Mesopotamian creation narratives such as the Enuma Elish (18th century BCE), where creation results from divine conflict.

Theological Implications

The theology of creation establishes: (1) Radical monotheism; (2) Creation ex nihilo; (3) Intrinsic goodness of creation; (4) Absolute sovereignty without cosmic opposition.

Archaeological Evidence

Discoveries at Ugarit (Ras Shamra) and Ebla illuminate Canaanite cosmogonic language, showing how Genesis employs common terms but strips them of mythological connotations.

"The word which came unto Jeremiah from Jehovah, when Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, and all his army, and all the kingdoms of the earth that were under his dominion, and all the peoples, were fighting against Jerusalem, and against all the cities thereof, saying:"
1.ninos:
🌌 The Great Beginning! 🌍

Hello, universe explorers! 🚀 Imagine: before there was anything... not even time! ⏰... God decided to CREATE EVERYTHING! ✨

With a powerful "LET THERE BE!" 🤓, God made appear from absolute nothing ✨ the blue skies full of twinkling stars ⭐ and the Earth with mountains 🏔️, rivers 🌊 and animals 🐶. Like the greatest magic ever! 🪄

God is the most amazing artist 🎨 and everything is His masterpiece. Including YOU! 😊 When you see the clouds ☁️ or play in the park 🌳, remember: it all started with Gods creative love! ❤️

"The word which came unto Jeremiah from Jehovah, when Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, and all his army, and all the kingdoms of the earth that were under his dominion, and all the peoples, were fighting against Jerusalem, and against all the cities thereof, saying:"

10.normal:
Devotional Study of Jeremiah 34:10

The first verse establishes the foundation of all biblical revelation: God as sovereign Creator. The phrase "In the beginning" places us at the inaugural moment of creation, not as an event within time, but as the beginning of time itself. God is not part of creation, but its absolute origin.

The verb "created" (from the Hebrew bara) is exclusive to God in the Scriptures, indicating creation ex nihilo (from nothing). This is not about transforming pre-existing matter, but calling into existence what did not exist before. The expression "the heavens and the earth" encompasses the totality of created reality.

This verse answers fundamental questions: Where do we come from? Who is in control? The answer is clear: everything comes from God and depends on Him. As believers, this truth gives us security in divine sovereignty over our lives.

"And all the princes and all the people obeyed, that had entered into the covenant, that every one should let his man-servant, and every one his maid-servant, go free, that none should make bondmen of them any more; they obeyed, and let them go:"
10.academico:
Exegetical Analysis of Jeremiah 34:10

Philological Analysis

The original Hebrew text presents crucial elements for exegesis. Bereshit (בְּרֵאשִׁית) is a prepositional construction meaning "in the beginning of", indicating the absolute beginning. The verb bara (בָּרָא) appears in perfect tense (qatal), denoting completed action. This verb has God as its exclusive subject in the Tanakh, distinguishing it from asah (to make) and yatsar (to form).

Historical Context

Source criticism identifies this opening with the Priestly tradition (P), dated to the exilic or post-exilic period (6th-5th century BCE). This contrasts with Mesopotamian creation narratives such as the Enuma Elish (18th century BCE), where creation results from divine conflict.

Theological Implications

The theology of creation establishes: (1) Radical monotheism; (2) Creation ex nihilo; (3) Intrinsic goodness of creation; (4) Absolute sovereignty without cosmic opposition.

Archaeological Evidence

Discoveries at Ugarit (Ras Shamra) and Ebla illuminate Canaanite cosmogonic language, showing how Genesis employs common terms but strips them of mythological connotations.

"And all the princes and all the people obeyed, that had entered into the covenant, that every one should let his man-servant, and every one his maid-servant, go free, that none should make bondmen of them any more; they obeyed, and let them go:"
10.ninos:
🌌 The Great Beginning! 🌍

Hello, universe explorers! 🚀 Imagine: before there was anything... not even time! ⏰... God decided to CREATE EVERYTHING! ✨

With a powerful "LET THERE BE!" 🤓, God made appear from absolute nothing ✨ the blue skies full of twinkling stars ⭐ and the Earth with mountains 🏔️, rivers 🌊 and animals 🐶. Like the greatest magic ever! 🪄

God is the most amazing artist 🎨 and everything is His masterpiece. Including YOU! 😊 When you see the clouds ☁️ or play in the park 🌳, remember: it all started with Gods creative love! ❤️

"And all the princes and all the people obeyed, that had entered into the covenant, that every one should let his man-servant, and every one his maid-servant, go free, that none should make bondmen of them any more; they obeyed, and let them go:"

11.texto_original: but afterwards they turned, and caused the servants and the handmaids, whom they had let go free, to return, and brought them into subjection for servants and for handmaids.

Devotional Study of Jeremiah 34:19

The first verse establishes the foundation of all biblical revelation: God as sovereign Creator. The phrase "In the beginning" places us at the inaugural moment of creation, not as an event within time, but as the beginning of time itself. God is not part of creation, but its absolute origin.

The verb "created" (from the Hebrew bara) is exclusive to God in the Scriptures, indicating creation ex nihilo (from nothing). This is not about transforming pre-existing matter, but calling into existence what did not exist before. The expression "the heavens and the earth" encompasses the totality of created reality.

This verse answers fundamental questions: Where do we come from? Who is in control? The answer is clear: everything comes from God and depends on Him. As believers, this truth gives us security in divine sovereignty over our lives.

"the princes of Judah, and the princes of Jerusalem, the eunuchs, and the priests, and all the people of the land, that passed between the parts of the calf;"
19.academico:
Exegetical Analysis of Jeremiah 34:19

Philological Analysis

The original Hebrew text presents crucial elements for exegesis. Bereshit (בְּרֵאשִׁית) is a prepositional construction meaning "in the beginning of", indicating the absolute beginning. The verb bara (בָּרָא) appears in perfect tense (qatal), denoting completed action. This verb has God as its exclusive subject in the Tanakh, distinguishing it from asah (to make) and yatsar (to form).

Historical Context

Source criticism identifies this opening with the Priestly tradition (P), dated to the exilic or post-exilic period (6th-5th century BCE). This contrasts with Mesopotamian creation narratives such as the Enuma Elish (18th century BCE), where creation results from divine conflict.

Theological Implications

The theology of creation establishes: (1) Radical monotheism; (2) Creation ex nihilo; (3) Intrinsic goodness of creation; (4) Absolute sovereignty without cosmic opposition.

Archaeological Evidence

Discoveries at Ugarit (Ras Shamra) and Ebla illuminate Canaanite cosmogonic language, showing how Genesis employs common terms but strips them of mythological connotations.

"the princes of Judah, and the princes of Jerusalem, the eunuchs, and the priests, and all the people of the land, that passed between the parts of the calf;"
19.ninos:
🌌 The Great Beginning! 🌍

Hello, universe explorers! 🚀 Imagine: before there was anything... not even time! ⏰... God decided to CREATE EVERYTHING! ✨

With a powerful "LET THERE BE!" 🤓, God made appear from absolute nothing ✨ the blue skies full of twinkling stars ⭐ and the Earth with mountains 🏔️, rivers 🌊 and animals 🐶. Like the greatest magic ever! 🪄

God is the most amazing artist 🎨 and everything is His masterpiece. Including YOU! 😊 When you see the clouds ☁️ or play in the park 🌳, remember: it all started with Gods creative love! ❤️

"the princes of Judah, and the princes of Jerusalem, the eunuchs, and the priests, and all the people of the land, that passed between the parts of the calf;"

20.texto_original: I will even give them into the hand of their enemies, and into the hand of them that seek their life; and their dead bodies shall be for food unto the birds of the heavens, and to the beasts of the earth.

21.texto_original: And Zedekiah king of Judah and his princes will I give into the hand of their enemies, and into the hand of them that seek their life, and into the hand of the king of Babylon's army, that are gone away from you.

22.texto_original: Behold, I will command, saith Jehovah, and cause them to return to this city; and they shall fight against it, and take it, and burn it with fire: and I will make the cities of Judah a desolation, without inhabitant.
22.normal:
Devotional Study of Jeremiah 34:22

"Behold, I will command, saith Jehovah, and cause them to return to this city; and they shall fight against it, and take it, and burn it with fire: and I will make the cities of Judah a desolation, without inhabitant."
